Research Pipeline · DistilBERT · SST-2 / MNLI

LLM Compression
Lab

A full-stack compression and fine-tuning pipeline — from raw FP32 to sparse, quantized, distilled transformer models.

91%
FP32 baseline accuracy
1.9×
Size reduction (INT8)
1.30×
Inference speedup
90.9%
Best compressed accuracy
Overview

What We Built

This lab applies six compression and adaptation techniques to DistilBERT (66M parameters), measuring their effect on accuracy, model size, latency, and throughput at each stage.

01

FP32 Baseline

Load distilbert-base-uncased-finetuned-sst-2-english. Establish 91.06% accuracy, 267.9 MB, 68.9 ms latency on CPU.

02

Dynamic PTQ INT8

Weight-only dynamic quantization via torch.ao. 1.93× size reduction, only −1.38% accuracy loss.

03

Layerwise Sensitivity Analysis

Quantize-then-restore method across 38 linear layers. Identifies that 2 layers drive >90% of accuracy loss.

04

Mixed-Precision Policy

4 layers → FP32, 2 → INT8, 32 → INT4 (84% of layers compressed to INT4).

05

Magnitude Pruning + INT8

L1 unstructured pruning before quantization. 30% sparsity: only −0.46% accuracy at same 1.30× speedup.

06

LoRA Fine-Tuning

Adapt to MNLI with r=8, only 739K trainable parameters (1.1% of model). 66% accuracy in 3 epochs.

07

Knowledge Distillation

FP32 teacher → LoRA student. T=4, α=0.7 recovers to 90.94% — just 0.12% below FP32.

Notebook 01 · Compression

Quantization Results

Dynamic PTQ INT8 is the correct approach for BERT-family models: HuggingFace transformers lack QuantStub/DeQuantStub in their forward pass, so static quantization is incompatible. Weights are quantized at load time; activations remain FP32 at runtime.

Method Task Accuracy Size Δ Acc Latency @bs=1 Throughput @bs=32 Speedup
FP32 (CPU) SST-2 91.06% 267.9 MB — 68.9 ms 17.2 s/s —
PTQ INT8 SST-2 89.68% 138.7 MB −1.38% 53.1 ms 20.3 s/s 1.30×
Mixed-precision SST-2 89.68% 138.7 MB −1.38% 55.1 ms 20.4 s/s 1.25×
Sparse 30% + INT8 SST-2 90.60% 138.7 MB −0.46% 52.8 ms 20.4 s/s 1.30×
Sparse 50% + INT8 SST-2 87.84% 138.7 MB −3.21% 53.3 ms 20.6 s/s 1.29×
Figure 1 — Accuracy, model size, latency, and throughput across quantization and pruning methods (Notebook 01)
✦

Best accuracy-compression trade-off

Sparse 30% + INT8 achieves only −0.46% accuracy loss at 1.93× compression and 1.30× speedup. Pruning before quantization maps zeroed weights cleanly to the INT8 zero-point with no additional quantization error.

§4 · Sensitivity Analysis

Layerwise Quantization Sensitivity

The quantize-then-restore method temporarily swaps each linear layer back to FP32, measures the accuracy delta on 256 validation samples, then restores INT8. High delta means the layer is sensitive and should be protected at higher precision.

🔴

High Sensitivity

2 layers with Δ > 1.0% — these drive the majority of quantization accuracy loss.

+1.95% layer.2.ffn.lin2
🟠

Medium Sensitivity

2 layers with Δ between 0.3–1.0% — assigned INT8 in the mixed-precision policy.

+0.39% layer.3.attention.v_lin
🔵

Low Sensitivity

34 layers with Δ ≈ 0% — safe INT4 candidates. 84% of all linear layers fall here.

84% of layers are INT4-safe
Figure 2 — Per-layer accuracy delta when restored to FP32. INT8 baseline: 89.84%. Bars colored by sensitivity tier.
✦

Sensitivity is highly concentrated

Just 2 of 38 linear layers (layer.2.ffn.lin2 and layer.1.attention.q_lin) account for nearly all quantization-induced accuracy loss. This justifies mixed-precision: protect only those layers in FP32 and compress everything else aggressively.

§6 · Magnitude Pruning

Sparse INT8 Results

L1 unstructured pruning zeros out the smallest weights by absolute value. Pruning happens before INT8 quantization — zeroed weights map exactly to the quantization zero-point, incurring no additional error.

⚡

30% Sparsity

Best trade-off. Only −0.46% accuracy vs FP32 at 1.93× smaller model. Same throughput gain as vanilla INT8.

90.60% accuracy (−0.46%)
📉

50% Sparsity

Diminishing returns. −3.21% accuracy loss for the same model size, since unstructured sparsity doesn't unlock hardware speedup without sparse kernel support.

87.84% accuracy (−3.21%)
§7 · Activation Analysis

FP32 vs INT8 Activation Distributions

Forward hooks capture output activations from 6 attention and FFN layers. KL divergence quantifies distribution shift due to quantization. KL > 0.01 would signal a meaningful shift.

Figure 3 — FP32 (blue) vs INT8 (orange) activation distributions. All KL values are between 0.0003 and 0.0017 — well below the 0.01 risk threshold.
✦

Dynamic INT8 preserves activation distributions

All KL divergences are <0.002 across all sampled layers, confirming that dynamic quantization faithfully preserves the FP32 activation distributions. The σ values differ by <1% in every case.

Notebook 02 · Fine-Tuning

LoRA Fine-Tuning on MNLI

LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) inserts trainable rank-decomposition matrices alongside frozen base weights. With r=8 and alpha=16, only 739,586 parameters are trainable — 1.1% of DistilBERT's 66M.

Figure 4 — LoRA training loss and val accuracy over 3 epochs on MNLI (r=8, alpha=16)
Figure 5 — Gradient norm distribution for LoRA adapters and frozen base layers. No vanishing or exploding gradients detected.
✦

Healthy gradient flow

All gradient norms fall between 1e−6 and 10. The adapter and classification head layers show significantly higher gradient norms than frozen base layers (as expected), confirming that only the intended parameters are updating.

from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model

config = LoraConfig(
    r=8,
    lora_alpha=16,
    target_modules=["q_lin", "v_lin"],
    lora_dropout=0.1,
)
model = get_peft_model(base_model, config)
# Trainable: 739,586 / 66,955,010 = 1.1%
§5–7 · Knowledge Distillation

Distillation Sweeps

The combined distillation loss balances soft teacher knowledge (KL divergence, scaled by T²) and hard task supervision (cross-entropy):

L = alpha × KL(softmax(student/T) ‖ softmax(teacher/T)) × T²
+ (1 − alpha) × CrossEntropy(student, true_labels)

The T² scaling preserves gradient magnitude as temperature increases — without it, the soft loss contribution collapses.

Figure 6 — Distillation training: total loss, hard (CE) loss, and soft (KL×T²) loss over 3 epochs. T=4.0, α=0.7.
Figure 7 — Temperature sweep (T = 2, 4, 8). T=4 achieves the best accuracy recovery at 91.17%.
Figure 8 — Alpha sweep at T=4. α=0.5 matches teacher accuracy exactly; α=0.7 is standard practice.
Temperature Best Accuracy vs Baseline Final Loss Verdict
T = 4.0 91.17% +0.11% 0.148 Best
T = 8.0 91.06% 0.00% 0.161 Good
T = 2.0 90.71% −0.34% 0.132 Suboptimal
✦

T=4 is the optimal temperature

Low T=2 keeps distributions sharp — soft labels carry little extra signal beyond the argmax. High T=8 over-flattens distributions, flooding the student with noise. T=4 strikes the right balance: soft enough to transfer inter-class information, sharp enough to maintain meaningful signal.

Final Benchmark · All Variants

Cross-Pipeline Results

The complete picture across both notebooks. Note that LoRA models are adapted for MNLI (3-class NLI) rather than SST-2 (binary sentiment), so lower accuracy reflects task difficulty, not model failure.

Method Task Accuracy Size Latency @bs=1 Throughput @bs=32 Speedup
FP32 baseline SST-2 91.06% 267.9 MB 69.6 ms 16.6 s/s —
PTQ INT8 SST-2 89.68% 138.7 MB 53.7 ms 18.8 s/s 1.30×
LoRA FP32 (r=8) MNLI 66.02% 270.2 MB 71.4 ms 17.1 s/s —
LoRA + QAT INT8 MNLI 64.40% 139.3 MB 70.6 ms 17.0 s/s —
Distillation (T=4, α=0.7) SST-2 90.94% 270.9 MB 73.5 ms 17.0 s/s −0.12%
Figure 9 — Accuracy, model size, latency, and throughput across all pipeline variants (Notebook 02)
🏆

Best Compression

PTQ INT8 and Sparse 30% + INT8 both achieve 1.93× size reduction. Sparse 30% wins on accuracy at 90.60%.

1.93× smaller than FP32
🎯

Best Accuracy Recovery

Knowledge distillation at T=4 recovers to 90.94%, just 0.12% below FP32, without any compression overhead.

−0.12% vs FP32 baseline
⚡

Best Speedup

All INT8 variants achieve 1.30× single-batch latency speedup on CPU. Throughput gains reach 20%+ at batch size 32.

1.30× faster inference
LLM Compression Lab · DistilBERT · SST-2 / MNLI
Tesla T4 GPU (training) · CPU inference benchmarks · 100 timed runs + 10 warm-up
Techniques: PTQ · Mixed-Precision · Pruning · LoRA · QAT · Distillation
Frameworks: PyTorch · HuggingFace · PEFT · ONNXRuntime
